common_name: Knock Out Rose (Red Knock Out Rose)

planting.note: Plant container shrubs in late winter, spring, or fall in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic (pH 6.0–6.5), fertile soil in full sun (minimum 6 hours direct sun); Knock Out roses require at least 6 hours of sun for adequate bloom production — do not plant in partial shade.

directions: Plant in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic, fertile soil in full sun; Knock Out rose is the single most significant development in landscape roses in the last century — introduced in 2000 by breeder William Radler after eighteen years of breeding for disease resistance and repeat bloom, it completely transformed the landscape rose market and is estimated to be the best-selling rose in American history with over 80 million plants sold in the first decade after introduction. The cherry-red, single flowers are produced in prolific, continuous flushes from spring until hard frost in zone 9 — a bloom frequency and duration that no traditional hybrid tea, floribunda, or shrub rose approaches. More importantly, Knock Out is essentially disease-immune: it shows extraordinary natural resistance to black spot (the primary rose disease in humid climates), powdery mildew, and rose rosette disease at levels that allow it to be grown without fungicide sprays in virtually all regions of the United States. In zone 9, the cherry-red flowers bloom through the entire warm season with brief pauses between flushes. Prune in late winter (February in zone 9) by cutting the entire plant back to 12–18 inches to stimulate vigorous new growth and maximum bloom. No deadheading is required — the plant is self-cleaning.
sources: Star Roses and Plants — Knock Out Rose Breeder Records

common_name: Double Knock Out Rose (Double Red Knock Out Rose)

planting.note: Plant container shrubs in late winter, spring, or fall in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic (pH 6.0–6.5), fertile soil in full sun (minimum 6 hours direct sun); Double Knock Out has the same exceptional disease resistance, heat tolerance, and low-maintenance requirements as the original Knock Out.

directions: Plant in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic, fertile soil in full sun; Double Knock Out rose is the most popular cultivar in the Knock Out series and is now the best-selling landscape rose in America — a double-flowered sport of the original Knock Out selected for its lush, fully double, deep-red to cherry-red flowers that more closely resemble a traditional garden rose than the single-petaled original. While the original Knock Out has 5 petals in the typical wild-rose single form, Double Knock Out produces 18–25 petals in a cupped, decorative double form that most gardeners associate with 'real' roses. This additional petal count gives the flowers a fuller, more luxurious appearance while maintaining essentially all the disease resistance, repeat bloom, and low-maintenance characteristics of the parent. Double Knock Out blooms from spring until hard frost in zone 9, self-cleaning without deadheading, and requires only one annual pruning in late winter (February in zone 9) cutting the plant back to 12–18 inches. Like the original, it shows exceptional black spot resistance — a critical trait in the humid Gulf Coast climate where most roses require weekly fungicide programs. The deep-red flower color is consistent through the season without fading. Numerous Knock Out series siblings exist: Pink Knock Out, Pink Double Knock Out, Sunny Knock Out (yellow), Rainbow Knock Out (coral-pink), and others.
sources: Star Roses and Plants — Knock Out Rose Breeder Records

planting.note: Plant container plants in spring or fall in well-drained to dry, lean to moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral or alkaline soil in full sun; Autumn sage requires excellent drainage — it will not tolerate wet feet and is extremely susceptible to root rot in heavy clay or wet soil.

directions: Plant in well-drained to dry, lean to moderately fertile, slightly acidic to alkaline soil in full sun; Autumn sage is a tough, drought-tolerant native Texas and New Mexico shrubby perennial that has become one of the most widely planted salvias for Gulf Coast gardens — blooming from spring through fall and into winter in zone 9 with red, pink, coral, or white flowers (depending on cultivar) that are intensely attractive to hummingbirds. The species has brilliant red tubular flowers; named cultivars extend the color range to salmon, coral, bicolor, and white. 'Furman's Red' is the standard deep-red selection; 'Cherry Chief' has deeper cherry-red; 'Maraschino' is vivid scarlet; 'Peach' and 'Coral' extend into warm pastel ranges. Autumn sage is a semi-woody subshrub — the woody base persists through winter while the upper stems may be killed by hard freezes and regenerate from the base in spring. Unlike many salvias that go dormant in zone 9 heat, Autumn sage continues blooming through the hottest summer months with some reduction in activity during the peak weeks. The combination of summer-to-frost bloom, hummingbird attraction, and remarkable drought tolerance makes it one of the most valuable and reliable perennials for full-sun Gulf Coast gardens. Trim lightly after each bloom flush to encourage reblooming.

common_name: Santa Barbara Mexican Bush Sage (Compact Mexican Sage)

planting.note: Plant container divisions in spring in well-drained, lean to moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Santa Barbara is a compact selection that grows about half the size of the standard Salvia leucantha and is suitable for smaller garden spaces where the full-size species would overwhelm.

directions: Plant in well-drained, lean to moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Santa Barbara is a compact selection of Mexican bush sage (Salvia leucantha) — a smaller, more garden-friendly alternative to the full-size species that can reach 4–6 feet. Santa Barbara stays 2–3 feet tall and wide, making it practical for smaller beds and borders where the full-size species would quickly become overwhelming. Like the species, Santa Barbara produces spectacular, long (6–12 inch) velvety-purple and white flower spikes from late summer through fall — the velvety-purple calyxes enclose small white flowers in a distinctive bicolor spike that is exceptional for late-season pollinator value and cut-flower use. The long, arching, purple flower spikes of Mexican sage provide the single most dramatic fall display of any commonly grown Gulf Coast perennial — no other plant matches the intensity and quantity of the purple-and-white flower production in September through November. Santa Barbara's compact habit blooms slightly earlier than the full-size species, making it more practical in zone 9 where the species sometimes misses the first frost deadline. Cut back to 12 inches after blooming; the plant regrows vigorously from the woody base. Drought-tolerant, deer-resistant, and essentially maintenance-free in well-drained soil.

common_name: Jacob Cline Bee Balm (Red Bee Balm, Mildew-Resistant Bergamot)

planting.note: Plant divisions or container plants in spring in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, moderately fertile soil in full sun to partial shade; Jacob Cline spreads aggressively by underground stolons — divide every 2–3 years to prevent the colony from expanding beyond its intended area.

directions: Plant in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, moderately fertile soil in full sun to partial shade; Jacob Cline is widely considered the finest red bee balm cultivar for hot, humid climates — selected for its exceptional resistance to powdery mildew, the primary problem that disfigures most Monarda didyma cultivars in Gulf South summer conditions. Most bee balm cultivars develop severe powdery mildew by midsummer in the humidity of the Gulf Coast — white, powdery coating on the foliage that disfigures the plant for the remainder of the season. Jacob Cline was selected from a large planting for the plants that remained cleanest through humid summers, resulting in dramatically superior mildew resistance compared to standard bee balm selections. The large, intense scarlet-red flower heads are exceptionally attractive to hummingbirds, which hover around the tubular flowers seeking nectar — Jacob Cline is one of the finest hummingbird plants available for partial-shade gardens in zone 9. It also attracts bees, swallowtails, and sphinx moths. The aromatic, mint-scented foliage is characteristic of the mint family. Jacob Cline spreads vigorously by underground runners and should be divided every 2–3 years in early spring to contain the colony and maintain plant vigor. In zone 9, it blooms from May through August.
